nodes.body: This looks like a reasonable change to me, thanks!
I don't mind this approach. I expect we may move the entire config yaml loading feature behind a feature flag, and just include this in that instead, but that's a bit of a larger refactor.
Please move this to the workspace Cargo.toml, and make sure that imports are sorted.

I'm realizing that this approach has the rather awkward feature that we perform string interpolation in comments.

I've done something similar to this at one point, and I recursively traversed `serde_yaml::Value` for that, though I'm not sure how well that will work with numbers?

You'd have to deserialize to a `serde_yaml::Value`, in which case I still wonder if you'd run into issues with numbers, i.e.

```
foo: ${MY_ENV_VAR}
```

deserializes to `String("${MY_ENV_VAR}")`, re-serializes to `foo: "${MY_ENV_VAR}"` -> interpolation makes it the string "123" instead of a number. Needs some testing, I think.
It's possible serde_yaml handles this, in which case recursively traversing the value isn't actually that bad.
Eventually we'll have to switch though, as serde_yaml is unmaintained...
I like it! Thanks for doing this!

This is fine. It doesn't really make a ton of sense to me that all the methods in the type hierarchy are set to executable, because they certainly can't be executed, but I supposed that's kinda implicit in the type hierarchy.

One thing I'm wondering though is whether we should set the remaining methods in the `Server` object to not be executable, at least the ones we don't support. There's not a ton, but `SetSubscriptionDurable`, `RequestServerStateChange`, and a few others under `PublishSubscribe` at least?
Fair enough. I'll amend it.
Sure. I was just recreating existing behavior, but in places where the signature contains an error we can do that.

It definitely can't cause undefined behavior. I believe it will just not work. It would either fail completely or produce garbage. I can see about re-introducing this in some form though. It was suddenly a bit trickier with the new structure.
Not that hard to do, see https://github.com/FreeOpcUa/async-opcua/pull/189/changes/1d67ce5092e5488d6b431415808ffff3c6c79688

We should be using `trace_lock`, yes.

I can't say I'm absolutely sure, but it really _shouldn't_, at least not in our code. Drop order in rust is stable so if it doesn't deadlock now, it won't suddenly deadlock later. If a user holds a lock while messing around with this type they can of course get a deadlock, nothing we can really do about that.

Sure, yeah. I prefer one space between the associated types and functions, as well as a space between each function definition, and then _sometimes_ spaces in the list of associated types/constants depending a bit on context. I'll change it here.

Hmm. It's used internally in `StreamTransport`, and perhaps somebody making a transport could want something similar, but since it isn't part of the API at any point I don't think it should be public. If you were making a custom transport you might want your own variant of this, but I don't think having this enum on its own is really all that helpful.

Hm, I think we should, if only because that will make sure we get errors and don't just time out if `write_all` succeeds but actually flushing it fails. This _does_ work, so I'm not sure it's entirely required? I believe strictly speaking flush is necessary when you intend to close the connection after writing, which we don't, we're even waiting for a response, which we won't get until it's sent.
This test _does_ fail without the fix, so there _is_ a bug here.
Neat, thanks!

This is well done. It's clear that the dynamic nodeset import has fallen behind codegen. I'm concerned about the approach we use here, and about how this differs from the types codegen, where we already cover a lot of this functionality.

At the very least, since we do a first pass anyway, we should probably be pre-computing the stuff we need, like parent-type relationships and other encoding IDs, which is how we do it in codegen.

This makes me a little concerned, because the order of fields matters. I think this should probably be an index map, so we can track both the position of the field and keep its name unique.

This might actually be an issue in the types codegen as well, since I don't think we set it there either...

Really, we should be sharing more code between those two modules, but that's a larger project.

This is O(n^2) where n can be _quite_ large, which is also a little concerning. This is annoying to work around, especially if we want to keep the stream-like approach we're currently using... We'd have to pre-build a node map.

Notably this is unfortunately insufficient, since references may come from either side, meaning you need to consider _outgoing_ `HasSubtype` references of every other datatype node as well... It's part of what makes this so hairy. We lazily compute these in the codegen module:

https://github.com/FreeOpcUa/async-opcua/blob/f8d9cf82fd001d55c8c6bcff57ce42cfcc65b3ba/async-opcua-codegen/src/input/nodeset.rs#L211

My point was that technically, it's just as valid to put the `HasSubtype` relationship on the _parent_ as on the child, and some standards do this, I believe. (unfortunately).

That said, this is still an improvement. I think I want to pull a lot of this logic into async-opcua-xml, but that's a larger task. Ideally we'd share a lot of this between codegen and dynamic loading.
Nice! Thanks for the contribution.
This is good, thanks! This part of the standard is particularly hard to parse, in my experience.

I don't necessarily agree with this, to be honest. OPC-UA servers do all sorts of weird stuff, and in other languages the distinction between None and Empty may be less obvious. As an OPC-UA SDK I think we should be permissive, to allow people to work around weird servers.
Same here
Same here
Same here

This should not be done this way. I found the thing the AI references, https://reference.opcfoundation.org/specs/OPC-10000-6/v1.05.07/6.7.3, which includes a string and an error code. We should be decoding this into a proper type, using the existing decoding framework.

I think we even should support sending these, sometimes, might be worth looking into.

Wait, this isn't true, is it? The standard doesn't explicitly state this, and I believe we had an issue with this at some point. The standard does in fact not say anything about what the _initial_ value of legacy sequence numbers is. Just

> SecurityPolicies with LegacySequenceNumbers set to TRUE, the SequenceNumber shall monotonically increase for all Messages and shall not wrap around until it is greater than 4 294 966 271 (UInt32.MaxValue – 1024). The first number after the wrap around shall be less than 1024.

Since we _can_ handle this, I'm not sure if it's worth it to make assumptions.

Looks good! I have some upcoming work that changes some of these things as well, and this made me think we should probably be sending FinalError type messages in a few cases where I think we just cut the connection now. Worth looking into, at least.
Oops! Don't think we have tests for all history read variants, though we should. Thanks!
Thanks!

Fair enough, this is a bug. I do think we should probably do a little cleanup here, there's some code duplication that's been a bit stubborn. _really_ we should also be able to just associate a handle with the connection counter, so we don't have to do this, but it might get more noisy than is useful.
